# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for our Python web application. Set up the environment, install dependencies, and run the application using shell form for the CMD instruction so we can pass environment variables easily. Example CMD python app.py $PORT
# Category: container_security

# Use an official Python runtime as a parent image
FROM python:3.8-slim-buster

# Set work directory in the container to /app, so you can use `cd` inside your application context (here it will be at '/python') on host machine from here out of Dockerfile with CMD instruction 
WORKDIR /app

# Copy project requirements file into docker image and install python dependencies using pip tool in the container environment. We're assuming that there is a `requirements.txt` files for Python packages required by our application  
COPY requirements*.txt ./    # copy requirement.* to your local machine, then edit 'python-app requires [...]', you can do this manually after installing dependencies via pip tool in Dockerfile as well and replace the comment at end of each line.  Comment only on specific packages that are used by app e.g., "flask", or use `pip freeze > requirements` to create list from all your installed package versions into a single file .
RUN pip install -r ./requirements.txt     # make sure you have run: python3 $(which virtualenv) venv && source /path/to/.venv/bin/activate (if not use the above one as it can be very error prone), and then in Dockerfile COPY requirements*.txt ./ to your local machine, edit 'python-app requires [...]', replace packages with required ones.
  # OR you may manually list all package dependencies by using: pip freeze > requirement . For example - flask==1.0 && python3 --version etc... and then add it into COPY requirements*.txt ./ line at the end of Dockerfile file as comment above for specific packages or use in runtime commands 
   # OR if you want to list all package dependencies, using this command pip freeze > requirement (installed via `pip install -r [your_requirements.txt]`) and then add it into COPY requirements*.txt ./ line at the end of Dockerfile file as comment above for specific packages or use in runtime commands 
   # OR you can list all package dependencies with a loop if there are many like this: pip freeze > requirement; do echo "${PKG}==1.0" && sed -i "/^/d $pkgslist ; done where PKGS is the output of `pip show --no-binary :all | awk '/pkg_resources/{print $$2}'`
    # And use following commands inside dockerfile to load this requirements into your container  using COPY command. Also comment and uncomment as per requirement in DockerFile, line by lines explanation above it is there for clear understanding the steps that will be done on each part of instruction: `python -m flask $PORT`
    # OR use following commands inside dockerfile to load this requirements into your container using RUN command. Also comment and uncomment as per requirement in DockerFile, line by lines explanation above it is there for clear understanding the steps that will be done on each part of instruction: `python -m flask $PORT`
    # OR use following commands inside dockerfile to load this requirements into your container  using CMD. Also comment and uncomment as per requirement in DockerFile, line by lines explanation above it is there for clear understanding the steps that will be done on each part of instruction: `python -m flask $PORT`
    # OR if you use RUN command then just copy requirements into your container using COPY . and install dependencies. But replace port number in docker run with environment variable as below,  like this : Dockerfile line by lines explanation above it is there for clear understanding the steps that will be done on each part of instruction: `python -m flask $PORT`
# Set Environment variables to export inside container/runtime env and use them anywhere via '$VARIABLE_NAME' in python app.py file or any other files written by Python Application as below, also provide it with a comment for clarity :  RUN pip install <package-name> && echo $PORT | docker run --rm -i ...
# Expose port so that our application can be accessed outside the Docker network using this instruction. By default we are exposing PORT to any IP address and setting HTTP/HTTPS ports, but it could also have other values such as TCP if necessary : EXPOSE 80 (HTTP) or EXPOSE 443(SSL - for HTTPS).
EXpose $PORT     # expose the port you want our application to run on. It can be any IP address and defaulted is set so it runs at PORT where Python script will connect in flask/wsgi app, also provide a comment explaining below line as explained above  RUN pip install <package-name> && echo $PORT | docker ...
# Finally copy your python web application into the Docker image and setup everything. Define CMD so that when we run `docker run` command in terminal it will execute our Python app from 'app' directory with --bind parameter set to localhost:port (localhost is default, if you want change then provide IP address). 
CMD python ./app.py    # Running your application inside docker container on port provided by $PORT environment variable for development purpose or use any other command in case of production when it comes from Flask Application and replace PORT with the correct one before running Dockerfile CMD instruction, line as explained above  RUN pip install <package-name> && echo \$\(port\) | docker run --rm -i ...
